and would not burst from all its edges
like a star.
For there is no place
that does not see you. You must change your life.
excerpt: We could not know his legendary head, where the eyes once ripened. Yet his torso

title: Entrance
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
lines: Whoever you are step out in the evening
from your room where everything is known;
your house lies as the last thing before the distance.
Whoever you are.
With your eyes, barely freed
from the worn threshold,
you slowly lift a dark tree
and place it against the sky, slender and alone.
And you have made a world.
And it is vast and like a word
still ripening in silence.
And as your will takes hold of its meaning,
your eyes let go, gently.
stanzas: Whoever you are step out in the evening
from your room where everything is known;
your house lies as the last thing before the distance.
Whoever you are.
With your eyes, barely freed
from the worn threshold,
you slowly lift a dark tree
and place it against the sky, slender and alone.
And you have made a world.
And it is vast and like a word
still ripening in silence.
And as your will takes hold of its meaning,
your eyes let go, gently.
excerpt: Whoever you are step out in the evening from your room where everything is known;

title: I Am Much Too Alone in This World
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
lines: I am much too alone in this world, yet not alone enough
to make each hour holy.
I am much too small in this world, yet not small enough
to stand before you like a thing,
dark and intelligent.
I want my free will and want to accompany it
on roads that lead to action;
and in those quiet, hesitating times
when something approaches,
I want to be among those who know,
or else alone.
stanzas: I am much too alone in this world, yet not alone enough
to make each hour holy.
I am much too small in this world, yet not small enough
to stand before you like a thing,
dark and intelligent.
I want my free will and want to accompany it
on roads that lead to action;
and in those quiet, hesitating times
when something approaches,
I want to be among those who know,
or else alone.
excerpt: I am much too alone in this world, yet not alone enough to make each hour holy.
source: The Book of Hours (The Book of Monastic Life), 1899

title: Childhood
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
lines: It would happen this way. Through all those things
that one by one became clear,
one still moved as if among many snares
from school to home,
with a weight tied to each hand.
O hunger for being small again,
as one was once,
when everything had still begun,
and was all endless and unbroken.
Yet this was more than merely then.
Already then one spent the days
with figures and with games,
and with many things that happened,
and then with joy alone.
And at last from one larger joy
one sank down into one still larger.
stanzas: It would happen this way. Through all those things
that one by one became clear,
one still moved as if among many snares
from school to home,
with a weight tied to each hand.
O hunger for being small again,
as one was once,
when everything had still begun,
and was all endless and unbroken.
Yet this was more than merely then.
Already then one spent the days
with figures and with games,
and with many things that happened,
and then with joy alone.
And at last from one larger joy
one sank down into one still larger.
excerpt: It would happen this way. Through all those things that one by one became clear,

title: Love Song
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
lines: How shall I hold my soul
so that it does not touch yours?
How shall I lift it over you
toward other things?
Ah I would gladly shelter it
in darkness with a lost object,
in some strange quiet place
that does not tremble when your depths tremble.
Yet everything that touches us, you and me,
takes us together like a violin bow
drawing one voice from two strings.
On what instrument are we stretched?
What musician holds us in his hand?
O sweetest song.
stanzas: How shall I hold my soul
so that it does not touch yours?
How shall I lift it over you
toward other things?
Ah I would gladly shelter it
in darkness with a lost object,
in some strange quiet place
that does not tremble when your depths tremble.
Yet everything that touches us, you and me,
takes us together like a violin bow
drawing one voice from two strings.
On what instrument are we stretched?
What musician holds us in his hand?
O sweetest song.
excerpt: How shall I hold my soul so that it does not touch yours?

title: You Darkness, from Which I Come
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
lines: You darkness from which I come,
I love you more than the flame
that limits the world,
shining a small circle
outside which no one can know.
But darkness holds everything:
forms and flames,
animals and me,
how it takes in powers and people,
and perhaps a great presence
moves beside me.
I believe in nights.
stanzas: You darkness from which I come,
I love you more than the flame
that limits the world,
shining a small circle
outside which no one can know.
But darkness holds everything:
forms and flames,
animals and me,
how it takes in powers and people,
and perhaps a great presence
moves beside me.
I believe in nights.
excerpt: You darkness from which I come, I love you more than the flame
source: The Book of Hours (The Book of Monastic Life), 1899

title: I Live My Life in Widening Circles
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
lines: I live my life in widening circles
that stretch over all things.
I may not complete this final one,
but I give myself to it.
I circle around God, around the ancient tower,
and I circle for thousands of years;
and still I do not know:
am I a falcon, a storm,
or a great song.
stanzas: I live my life in widening circles
that stretch over all things.
I may not complete this final one,
but I give myself to it.
I circle around God, around the ancient tower,
and I circle for thousands of years;
and still I do not know:
am I a falcon, a storm,
or a great song.
excerpt: I live my life in widening circles that stretch over all things.
source: The Book of Hours (The Book of Monastic Life), 1899

title: You, Neighbor God
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
lines: You, neighbor God, when I disturb you
with hard knocking in the long night,
it is because I scarcely hear you breathing,
and I know: you are alone in your room.
And if you need something there is no one
to bring water to your hand:
I am listening, always.
Give me a sign.
I am near.
Only a narrow wall stands between us,
and by chance; for it might happen:
a call from your mouth or mine,
and it would break,
without any noise.
stanzas: You, neighbor God, when I disturb you
with hard knocking in the long night,
it is because I scarcely hear you breathing,
and I know: you are alone in your room.
And if you need something there is no one
to bring water to your hand:
I am listening, always.
Give me a sign.
I am near.
Only a narrow wall stands between us,
and by chance; for it might happen:
a call from your mouth or mine,
and it would break,
without any noise.
excerpt: You, neighbor God, when I disturb you with hard knocking in the long night,
source: The Book of Hours (The Book of Monastic Life), 1899

title: Sonnets to Orpheus I, 1
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
lines: A tree rose there. O pure ascension.
O Orpheus sings. O tall tree in the ear.
And everything fell silent.
Yet in this silence new beginning, sign, and change appeared.
Creatures out of stillness pressed themselves clear,
out of den and nesting dark,
and it was shown that they were not afraid
from cunning, not from fear,
but from listening.
Roaring, crying, shrieking
seemed small in their hearts.
Where once scarcely a hut stood to receive this,
a hidden shelter made of darkest longing,
with an entryway whose posts still trembled,
you built a temple in their hearing.
stanzas: A tree rose there. O pure ascension.
O Orpheus sings. O tall tree in the ear.
And everything fell silent.
Yet in this silence new beginning, sign, and change appeared.
Creatures out of stillness pressed themselves clear,
out of den and nesting dark,
and it was shown that they were not afraid
from cunning, not from fear,
but from listening.
Roaring, crying, shrieking
seemed small in their hearts.
Where once scarcely a hut stood to receive this,
a hidden shelter made of darkest longing,
with an entryway whose posts still trembled,
you built a temple in their hearing.
excerpt: A tree rose there. O pure ascension. O Orpheus sings. O tall tree in the ear.

title: Sonnets to Orpheus I, 3
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
lines: A god can do it. But tell me how
should a man follow him through the narrow lyre?
His senses divide him.
At the crossing of two heart-paths there is no temple for Apollo.
Song, as you teach it, is not desire,
not pursuit of something finally achieved.
Song is existence.
Simple for the god.
But when are we?
And when does he turn earth and stars toward us?
This is not about loving, youth,
even if your voice forces open your mouth.
Learn to forget that you sang.
It fades.
True singing is another breath,
a breath about nothing.
A gust in god.
A wind.
stanzas: A god can do it. But tell me how
should a man follow him through the narrow lyre?
His senses divide him.
At the crossing of two heart-paths there is no temple for Apollo.
Song, as you teach it, is not desire,
not pursuit of something finally achieved.
Song is existence.
Simple for the god.
But when are we?
And when does he turn earth and stars toward us?
This is not about loving, youth,
even if your voice forces open your mouth.
Learn to forget that you sang.
It fades.
True singing is another breath,
a breath about nothing.
A gust in god.
A wind.
excerpt: A god can do it. But tell me how should a man follow him through the narrow lyre?

title: Sonnets to Orpheus I, 5
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
lines: Set up no monument.
Let the rose bloom for him each year.
For this is Orpheus.
His metamorphosis in one thing and another.
We should not trouble ourselves with other names.
Once and for all it is Orpheus
when there is song.
He comes and goes.
Is it not enough if he outlives,
at times, the bowl of roses by a few days?
O how he must vanish,
so that you can grasp it.
Though he himself fears disappearance,
while your word outruns his being,
he is already there where you cannot follow.
And his lyre never cages his hands.
stanzas: Set up no monument.
Let the rose bloom for him each year.
For this is Orpheus.
His metamorphosis in one thing and another.
We should not trouble ourselves with other names.
Once and for all it is Orpheus
when there is song.
He comes and goes.
Is it not enough if he outlives,
at times, the bowl of roses by a few days?
O how he must vanish,
so that you can grasp it.
Though he himself fears disappearance,
while your word outruns his being,
he is already there where you cannot follow.
And his lyre never cages his hands.
excerpt: Set up no monument. Let the rose bloom for him each year.

title: Sonnets to Orpheus I, 9
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
lines: Only he who raised the lyre
among shadows
may also guess
what endless praise is.
Only he who ate with the dead
from their poppy,
will not lose
its softest tone.
Though the reflection in the pool
often blurs before us:
know the image.
Only in the double realm
will the voices
be eternal and gentle.
stanzas: Only he who raised the lyre
among shadows
may also guess
what endless praise is.
Only he who ate with the dead
from their poppy,
will not lose
its softest tone.
Though the reflection in the pool
often blurs before us:
know the image.
Only in the double realm
will the voices
be eternal and gentle.
excerpt: Only he who raised the lyre among shadows

title: Sonnets to Orpheus I, 19
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
lines: Though the world keeps changing
like cloud-shapes,
all completion returns
to what has always been.
Above change and motion,
freer and wider,
your fore-song remains,
god with the lyre.
Suffering does not divide us,
love does not teach us finality,
and death is not our closing.
Only song over the earth
hallows and celebrates.
stanzas: Though the world keeps changing
like cloud-shapes,
all completion returns
to what has always been.
Above change and motion,
freer and wider,
your fore-song remains,
god with the lyre.
Suffering does not divide us,
love does not teach us finality,
and death is not our closing.
Only song over the earth
hallows and celebrates.
excerpt: Though the world keeps changing like cloud-shapes,

title: Sonnets to Orpheus II, 1
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
lines: Breathing, you invisible poem.
Again and again exchanged in pure space.
Counterweight in which I happen rhythmically.
Single wave whose gradual sea I am.
Most sparing of all possible seas,
space-winner.
How many of these places were already in me.
Some winds are like my son.
Do you recognize me, air,
you still full once with places that were mine?
You smooth bark,
roundness and leaf of my words.
And yet so little of all this remains.
Where to?
If now everything that was once me
lets itself pass by in you.
stanzas: Breathing, you invisible poem.
Again and again exchanged in pure space.
Counterweight in which I happen rhythmically.
Single wave whose gradual sea I am.
Most sparing of all possible seas,
space-winner.
How many of these places were already in me.
Some winds are like my son.
Do you recognize me, air,
you still full once with places that were mine?
You smooth bark,
roundness and leaf of my words.
And yet so little of all this remains.
Where to?
If now everything that was once me
lets itself pass by in you.
excerpt: Breathing, you invisible poem. Again and again exchanged in pure space.

title: Sonnets to Orpheus II, 10
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
lines: Everything calls us to celebrate.
No season refuses us.
Even decline glows
like a fruit accepting weight.
Be earth now,
and know what earth means.
Mature in your own body,
trusting the dark.
The dead are not gone.
They ask us quietly
to continue what they began:
transformation.
Praise this world to the angel,
not the unsayable one,
you cannot impress him with splendor.
Tell him things.
He will stand astonished.
stanzas: Everything calls us to celebrate.
No season refuses us.
Even decline glows
like a fruit accepting weight.
Be earth now,
and know what earth means.
Mature in your own body,
trusting the dark.
The dead are not gone.
They ask us quietly
to continue what they began:
transformation.
Praise this world to the angel,
not the unsayable one,
you cannot impress him with splendor.
Tell him things.
He will stand astonished.
excerpt: Everything calls us to celebrate. No season refuses us.

title: Sonnets to Orpheus II, 12
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
lines: Will transformation not be your work?
Earth, beloved, I will.
Believe me,
you no longer need your springtimes
to win me over.
One, ah one, is already too much for my blood.
Namelessly I have belonged to you,
from the beginning.
Always you were right,
and your holiest invention is death,
that intimate friend.
See, I live.
On what?
Neither childhood nor future grows less.
Superabundant existence rises
in my heart.
stanzas: Will transformation not be your work?
Earth, beloved, I will.
Believe me,
you no longer need your springtimes
to win me over.
One, ah one, is already too much for my blood.
Namelessly I have belonged to you,
from the beginning.
Always you were right,
and your holiest invention is death,
that intimate friend.
See, I live.
On what?
Neither childhood nor future grows less.
Superabundant existence rises
in my heart.
excerpt: Will transformation not be your work? Earth, beloved, I will.

title: Sonnets to Orpheus II, 13
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
lines: Be ahead of all parting,
as if it were already behind you,
like the winter that has just gone.
For among winters one is so endless
that, wintering through it,
your heart survives all seasons.
Be forever dead in Eurydice,
more gladly rise into relation,
into pure connection.
Here among the vanishing
be in the realm of decline
a ringing glass that shatters even as it rings.
Be and know at once
the condition of non-being,
the endless ground of your inner vibration,
so that you may accomplish it once.
To the spent and the dumb,
to the inventory of full nature,
add yourself as praise,
and destroy the number.
stanzas: Be ahead of all parting,
as if it were already behind you,
like the winter that has just gone.
For among winters one is so endless
that, wintering through it,
your heart survives all seasons.
Be forever dead in Eurydice,
more gladly rise into relation,
into pure connection.
Here among the vanishing
be in the realm of decline
a ringing glass that shatters even as it rings.
Be and know at once
the condition of non-being,
the endless ground of your inner vibration,
so that you may accomplish it once.
To the spent and the dumb,
to the inventory of full nature,
add yourself as praise,
and destroy the number.
excerpt: Be ahead of all parting, as if it were already behind you,

title: Sonnets to Orpheus II, 29
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
lines: Silent friend of many distances,
feel how your breathing enlarges space.
Let your presence ring
on the dark bell-wood.
Whatever feeds itself on you
will become strong.
Step into transformation in and out.
What is your most painful experience?
If drinking is bitter,
become wine.
In this night of excess,
be the magic power
at the crossing of your senses,
the meaning of their strange encounter.
And if the earthly forgot you,
say quietly to the silent earth: I flow.
Say to the rapid water: I am.
stanzas: Silent friend of many distances,
feel how your breathing enlarges space.
Let your presence ring
on the dark bell-wood.
Whatever feeds itself on you
will become strong.
Step into transformation in and out.
What is your most painful experience?
If drinking is bitter,
become wine.
In this night of excess,
be the magic power
at the crossing of your senses,
the meaning of their strange encounter.
And if the earthly forgot you,
say quietly to the silent earth: I flow.
Say to the rapid water: I am.
excerpt: Silent friend of many distances, feel how your breathing enlarges space.

title: When thoughts run out
author: Ryokan
lines: When thoughts run out
I step into the woods
and gather
shepherd's purse.
stanzas: When thoughts run out
I step into the woods
and gather
shepherd's purse.
excerpt: When thoughts run out I step into the woods

title: Like a small stream
author: Ryokan
lines: Like a small stream
through mossy stone,
I keep going quietly
until I turn clear.
stanzas: Like a small stream
through mossy stone,
I keep going quietly
until I turn clear.
excerpt: Like a small stream through mossy stone,

title: At dusk I climb Kugami
author: Ryokan
lines: At dusk I climb Kugami.
A deer calls.
Its voice sinks
into fallen maple leaves
at the mountain's foot.
stanzas: At dusk I climb Kugami.
A deer calls.
Its voice sinks
into fallen maple leaves
at the mountain's foot.
excerpt: At dusk I climb Kugami. A deer calls.

title: How could I sleep in this moonlight?
author: Ryokan
lines: How could I sleep in this moonlight?
Friends, come.
Let us sing and dance
until dawn.
stanzas: How could I sleep in this moonlight?
Friends, come.
Let us sing and dance
until dawn.
excerpt: How could I sleep in this moonlight? Friends, come.

title: A little drunk
author: Ryokan
lines: A little drunk,
lying under the huge sky,
I dream beautifully
beneath cherry blossoms.
stanzas: A little drunk,
lying under the huge sky,
I dream beautifully
beneath cherry blossoms.
excerpt: A little drunk, lying under the huge sky,

title: Wild roses from the field
author: Ryokan
lines: Wild roses from the field,
frogs calling everywhere.
Float the petals in wine.
Enjoy this brief hour.
stanzas: Wild roses from the field,
frogs calling everywhere.
Float the petals in wine.
Enjoy this brief hour.
excerpt: Wild roses from the field, frogs calling everywhere.

title: Spring will open every branch
author: Ryokan
lines: Spring will open every branch.
Flowers will return.
But the children
who fell like last autumn leaves
will not come back.
stanzas: Spring will open every branch.
Flowers will return.
But the children
who fell like last autumn leaves
will not come back.
excerpt: Spring will open every branch. Flowers will return.

title: The wind is still
author: Ryokan
lines: The wind is still.
Blossoms are gone.
Birdsong deepens as mountains darken.
This, too, is the Dharma.
stanzas: The wind is still.
Blossoms are gone.
Birdsong deepens as mountains darken.
This, too, is the Dharma.
excerpt: The wind is still. Blossoms are gone.

title: The thief left one thing behind
author: Ryokan
lines: The thief left one thing behind:
the moon
at my window.
stanzas: The thief left one thing behind:
the moon
at my window.
excerpt: The thief left one thing behind: the moon

title: The wind gives me enough fallen leaves
author: Ryokan
lines: The wind gives me enough fallen leaves
to keep a small fire going.
stanzas: The wind gives me enough fallen leaves
to keep a small fire going.
excerpt: The wind gives me enough fallen leaves to keep a small fire going.

title: After many years away
author: Ryokan
lines: After many years away,
I return to my village,
sick,
and sleep in an inn.
One robe.
One bowl.
I burn incense
and sit through the rain.
All night the drizzle keeps falling.
Inside the dark room,
old journeys come back one by one.
stanzas: After many years away,
I return to my village,
sick,
and sleep in an inn.
One robe.
One bowl.
I burn incense
and sit through the rain.
All night the drizzle keeps falling.
Inside the dark room,
old journeys come back one by one.
excerpt: After many years away, I return to my village,

title: An old grave at the foot of a quiet hill
author: Ryokan
lines: An old grave at the foot of a quiet hill,
weeds high,
no one tending it.
Only a woodcutter passes sometimes.
I was his student once,
a rough-haired boy by the Narrow River.
Then I left,
and years went silent between us.
Now I pour clear water on his stone
and bow.
The sun drops behind the ridge,
wind fills the pines,
and I cannot walk away.
My sleeves are wet with tears.
stanzas: An old grave at the foot of a quiet hill,
weeds high,
no one tending it.
Only a woodcutter passes sometimes.
I was his student once,

title: When I was young
author: Ryokan
lines: When I was young
I put books aside
and wanted to be a saint.
I wandered many springs as a beggar monk.
At last I came home
and built a hut under a rough cliff.
Birdsong became my music.
Clouds became my neighbors.
Below: a clear spring.
Above: pines and oaks for shade and firewood.
Day after day,
free,
I have no wish to leave.
stanzas: When I was young
I put books aside
and wanted to be a saint.
I wandered many springs as a beggar monk.
At last I came home
and built a hut under a rough cliff.

title: Yes, I am a fool
author: Ryokan
lines: Yes, I am a fool,
living among trees and grass.
Do not ask me about illusion or enlightenment.
I only smile to myself.
I wade streams on thin legs,
carry my bag through spring weather,
and keep walking.
The world owes me nothing.
stanzas: Yes, I am a fool,
living among trees and grass.
Do not ask me about illusion or enlightenment.
I only smile to myself.
I wade streams on thin legs,
carry my bag through spring weather,
and keep walking.
The world owes me nothing.
excerpt: Yes, I am a fool, living among trees and grass.

title: Snow blends with wind
author: Ryokan
lines: Snow blends with wind.
Wind blends with snow.
By the hearth I stretch my legs,
doing nothing,
kept inside this hut.
Counting days,
I see even February
has already passed,
like a dream.
stanzas: Snow blends with wind.
Wind blends with snow.
By the hearth I stretch my legs,
doing nothing,
kept inside this hut.
Counting days,
I see even February

excerpt: Snow blends with wind. Wind blends with snow.

title: No luck on my begging rounds today
author: Ryokan
lines: No luck on my begging rounds today.
From village to village
I dragged this body.
By sunset,
mountains still stand between me and home.
The wind cuts through my robe.
My small bowl looks emptier than ever.
Still,
this is the path I chose:
cold,
hunger,
disappointment,
and a strange peace inside them.
stanzas: No luck on my begging rounds today.

title: I found this cracked wooden bowl in bamboo
author: Ryokan
lines: I found this cracked wooden bowl in bamboo,
washed it in spring water,
and mended it.
After meditation
it holds morning gruel.
At night,
soup or rice.
Worn,
split,
weathered,
out of shape—
still,
it is of noble stock.
stanzas: I found this cracked wooden bowl in bamboo,
washed it in spring water,
and mended it.

it holds morning gruel.
At night,
soup or rice.

out of shape—

title: In midsummer I walk with my staff
author: Ryokan
lines: In midsummer I walk with my staff.
Old farmers see me
and call me over for a drink.
We sit in the fields,
using leaves as plates.
Warm with sake,
happy,
I fall asleep
on the edge of a rice paddy.
stanzas: In midsummer I walk with my staff.
Old farmers see me
and call me over for a drink.
We sit in the fields,
using leaves as plates.
Warm with sake,
happy,
I fall asleep
on the edge of a rice paddy.
excerpt: In midsummer I walk with my staff. Old farmers see me

title: I watch people in this world
author: Ryokan
lines: I watch people in this world
wear themselves out
chasing things.
Desire never fills.
Even when it does,
joy vanishes quickly,
and the grip tightens.
Like monkeys reaching for the moon in water,
they fall into whirlpools.
All night I think of them,
unable to stop these tears.
stanzas: I watch people in this world
wear themselves out
chasing things.
Desire never fills.
Even when it does,
joy vanishes quickly,
and the grip tightens.
Like monkeys reaching for the moon in water,
they fall into whirlpools.
All night I think of them,
unable to stop these tears.
excerpt: I watch people in this world wear themselves out

title: In this broken three-room hut
author: Ryokan
lines: In this broken three-room hut
I have grown old.
This winter is the hardest yet.
I sip thin gruel
and wait through the freezing night.
Will I last until spring?
I cannot go out to beg for rice.
How will I survive this cold?
Even meditation gives little warmth now.
So I write poems
for friends who have already gone.
stanzas: In this broken three-room hut
I have grown old.

and wait through the freezing night.

title: Brahma
author: Ralph Waldo Emerson
lines: If the red slayer think he slays,
Or if the slain think he is slain,
They know not well the subtle ways
I keep, and pass, and turn again.
Far or forgot to me is near;
Shadow and sunlight are the same;
The vanished gods to me appear;
And one to me are shame and fame.
They reckon ill who leave me out;
When me they fly, I am the wings;
I am the doubter and the doubt,
And I the hymn the Brahmin sings.
The strong gods pine for my abode,
And pine in vain the sacred Seven;
But thou, meek lover of the good!
Find me, and turn thy back on heaven.
stanzas: If the red slayer think he slays,
Or if the slain think he is slain,
They know not well the subtle ways
I keep, and pass, and turn again.
Far or forgot to me is near;

And one to me are shame and fame.
They reckon ill who leave me out;

And I the hymn the Brahmin sings.
The strong gods pine for my abode,
And pine in vain the sacred Seven;
But thou, meek lover of the good!
Find me, and turn thy back on heaven.

title: The Eagle
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
lines: He clasps the crag with crooked hands;
Close to the sun in lonely lands,
Ring'd with the azure world, he stands.
The wrinkled sea beneath him crawls;
He watches from his mountain walls,
And like a thunderbolt he falls.
stanzas: He clasps the crag with crooked hands;
Close to the sun in lonely lands,
Ring'd with the azure world, he stands.
The wrinkled sea beneath him crawls;
He watches from his mountain walls,
And like a thunderbolt he falls.
excerpt: He clasps the crag with crooked hands; Close to the sun in lonely lands,

title: If We Must Die
author: Claude McKay
lines: If we must die—let it not be like hogs
Hunted and penned in an inglorious spot,
While round us bark the mad and hungry dogs,
Making their mock at our accursed lot.
If we must die—O let us nobly die,
So that our precious blood may not be shed
In vain; then even the monsters we defy
Shall be constrained to honor us though dead!
O kinsmen! we must meet the common foe!
Though far outnumbered let us show us brave,
And for their thousand blows deal one death-blow!
What though before us lies the open grave?
Like men we'll face the murderous, cowardly pack,
Pressed to the wall, dying, but fighting back!
stanzas: If we must die—let it not be like hogs
Hunted and penned in an inglorious spot,
While round us bark the mad and hungry dogs,

title: Holy Sonnet 10
author: John Donne
lines: Death, be not proud, though some have called thee
Mighty and dreadful, for thou art not so,
For those whom thou think'st thou dost overthrow
Die not, poor Death, nor yet canst thou kill me.
From rest and sleep, which but thy pictures be,
Much pleasure; then from thee much more must flow,
And soonest our best men with thee do go,
Rest of their bones and soul's delivery.
Thou art slave to fate, chance, kings, and desperate men,
And dost with poison, war, and sickness dwell,
And poppy or charms can make us sleep as well
And better than thy stroke; why swell'st thou then?
One short sleep past, we wake eternally,
And Death shall be no more; Death, thou shalt die.
stanzas: Death, be not proud, though some have called thee
Mighty and dreadful, for thou art not so,
For those whom thou think'st thou dost overthrow
Die not, poor Death, nor yet canst thou kill me.
From rest and sleep, which but thy pictures be,
Much pleasure; then from thee much more must flow,
And soonest our best men with thee do go,
Rest of their bones and soul's delivery.
Thou art slave to fate, chance, kings, and desperate men,
And dost with poison, war, and sickness dwell,
And poppy or charms can make us sleep as well
And better than thy stroke; why swell'st thou then?
One short sleep past, we wake eternally,
And Death shall be no more; Death, thou shalt die.
excerpt: Death, be not proud, though some have called thee Mighty and dreadful, for thou art not so,

title: To the Virgins, to Make Much of Time
author: Robert Herrick
lines: Gather ye rosebuds while ye may,
Old Time is still a-flying:
And this same flower that smiles today
Tomorrow will be dying.
The glorious lamp of heaven, the sun,
The higher he's a-getting,
The sooner will his race be run,
And nearer he's to setting.
That age is best which is the first,
When youth and blood are warmer;
But being spent, the worse, and worst
Times still succeed the former.
Then be not coy, but use your time,
And while ye may, go marry:
For having lost but once your prime,
You may forever tarry.
stanzas: Gather ye rosebuds while ye may,
Old Time is still a-flying:
And this same flower that smiles today
Tomorrow will be dying.
The glorious lamp of heaven, the sun,
The higher he's a-getting,
The sooner will his race be run,
And nearer he's to setting.
That age is best which is the first,

Then be not coy, but use your time,
And while ye may, go marry:
For having lost but once your prime,
You may forever tarry.
excerpt: Gather ye rosebuds while ye may, Old Time is still a-flying:

title: How Do I Love Thee?
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning
lines: How do I love thee? Let me count the ways.
I love thee to the depth and breadth and height
My soul can reach, when feeling out of sight
For the ends of Being and ideal Grace.
I love thee to the level of everyday's
Most quiet need, by sun and candle-light.
I love thee freely, as men strive for Right;
I love thee purely, as they turn from Praise.
I love thee with the passion put to use
In my old griefs, and with my childhood's faith.
I love thee with a love I seemed to lose
With my lost saints—I love thee with the breath,
Smiles, tears, of all my life!—and, if God choose,
I shall but love thee better after death.

I love thee to the depth and breadth and height
My soul can reach, when feeling out of sight

excerpt: How do I love thee? Let me count the ways. I love thee to the depth and breadth and height

title: First Fig
author: Edna St. Vincent Millay
lines: My candle burns at both ends;
It will not last the night;
But ah, my foes, and oh, my friends—
It gives a lovely light!
stanzas: My candle burns at both ends;
It will not last the night;
But ah, my foes, and oh, my friends—
It gives a lovely light!
excerpt: My candle burns at both ends; It will not last the night;

title: Mother to Son
author: Langston Hughes
lines: Well, son, I'll tell you:
Life for me ain't been no crystal stair.
It's had tacks in it,
And splinters,
And boards torn up,
And places with no carpet on the floor—
Bare.
But all the time
I'se been a-climbin' on,
And reachin' landin's,
And turnin' corners,
And sometimes goin' in the dark
Where there ain't been no light.
So boy, don't you turn back.
Don't you set down on the steps
'Cause you finds it's kinder hard.
Don't you fall now—
For I'se still goin', honey,
I'se still climbin',
And life for me ain't been no crystal stair.

Where there ain't been no light.

'Cause you finds it's kinder hard.
Don't you fall now—
For I'se still goin', honey,
I'se still climbin',
And life for me ain't been no crystal stair.
excerpt: Well, son, I'll tell you: Life for me ain't been no crystal stair.

title: Yet Do I Marvel
author: Countee Cullen
lines: I doubt not God is good, well-meaning, kind,
And did He stoop to quibble could tell why
The little buried mole continues blind,
Why flesh that mirrors Him must someday die,
Make plain the reason tortured Tantalus
Is baited by the fickle fruit, declare
If merely brute caprice dooms Sisyphus
To struggle up a never-ending stair.
Inscrutable His ways are, and immune
To catechism by a mind too strewn
With petty cares to slightly understand
What awful brain compels His awful hand.
Yet do I marvel at this curious thing:
To make a poet black, and bid him sing!
stanzas: I doubt not God is good, well-meaning, kind,
And did He stoop to quibble could tell why
The little buried mole continues blind,
Why flesh that mirrors Him must someday die,
Make plain the reason tortured Tantalus
Is baited by the fickle fruit, declare
If merely brute caprice dooms Sisyphus
To struggle up a never-ending stair.
Inscrutable His ways are, and immune
To catechism by a mind too strewn
With petty cares to slightly understand
What awful brain compels His awful hand.
Yet do I marvel at this curious thing:
To make a poet black, and bid him sing!
excerpt: I doubt not God is good, well-meaning, kind, And did He stoop to quibble could tell why

title: Coromandel Fishers
author: Sarojini Naidu
lines: Rise, brothers, rise; the wakening skies pray to the morning light,
The wind lies asleep in the arms of the dawn like a child that has cried all night.
Come, let us gather our nets from the shore, and set our catamarans free,
To capture the leaping wealth of the tide, for we are the kings of the sea!
No longer delay, let us hasten away in the track of the seagull's call,
The sea is our mother, the cloud is our brother, the waves are our comrades all;
What though we toss at the fall of the sun where the hand of the sea-god drives?
He who holds the storm by the hair, will hide in his breast our lives.
Sweet is the shade of the coconut glade, and the scent of the mango grove,
And sweet are the sands at the full o' the moon with the sound of the voices we love;
But sweeter, O brothers, the kiss of the spray and the dance of the wild foam's glee;
Row, brothers, row to the edge of the verge, where the low sky mates with the sea.
stanzas: Rise, brothers, rise; the wakening skies pray to the morning light,
The wind lies asleep in the arms of the dawn like a child that has cried all night.
Come, let us gather our nets from the shore, and set our catamarans free,
To capture the leaping wealth of the tide, for we are the kings of the sea!
No longer delay, let us hasten away in the track of the seagull's call,
The sea is our mother, the cloud is our brother, the waves are our comrades all;
What though we toss at the fall of the sun where the hand of the sea-god drives?
He who holds the storm by the hair, will hide in his breast our lives.
Sweet is the shade of the coconut glade, and the scent of the mango grove,
And sweet are the sands at the full o' the moon with the sound of the voices we love;
But sweeter, O brothers, the kiss of the spray and the dance of the wild foam's glee;
Row, brothers, row to the edge of the verge, where the low sky mates with the sea.
excerpt: Rise, brothers, rise; the wakening skies pray to the morning light, The wind lies asleep in the arms of the dawn like a child that has cried all night.

title: Fog
author: Carl Sandburg
lines: The fog comes
on little cat feet.
It sits looking
over harbor and city
on silent haunches
and then moves on.

title: Anecdote of the Jar
author: Wallace Stevens
lines: I placed a jar in Tennessee,
And round it was, upon a hill.
It made the slovenly wilderness
Surround that hill.
The wilderness rose up to it,
And sprawled around, no longer wild.
The jar was round upon the ground
And tall and of a port in air.
It took dominion everywhere.
The jar was gray and bare.
It did not give of bird or bush,
Like nothing else in Tennessee.

whirl your pointed pines,
splash your great pines
on our rocks,
hurl your green over us,
cover us with your pools of fir.

title: Suicide in the Trenches
author: Siegfried Sassoon
lines: I knew a simple soldier boy
Who grinned at life in empty joy,
Slept soundly through the lonesome dark,
And whistled early with the lark.
In winter trenches, cowed and glum,
With crumps and lice and lack of rum,
He put a bullet through his brain.
No one spoke of him again.
You smug-faced crowds with kindling eye
Who cheer when soldier lads march by,
Sneak home and pray you'll never know
The hell where youth and laughter go.
stanzas: I knew a simple soldier boy

title: Lift Every Voice and Sing
author: James Weldon Johnson
lines: Lift every voice and sing,
Till earth and heaven ring,
Ring with the harmonies of Liberty;
Let our rejoicing rise
High as the listening skies,
Let it resound loud as the rolling sea.
Sing a song full of the faith that the dark past has taught us,
Sing a song full of the hope that the present has brought us;
Facing the rising sun of our new day begun,
Let us march on till victory is won.
Stony the road we trod,
Bitter the chastening rod,
Felt in the days when hope unborn had died;
Yet with a steady beat,
Have not our weary feet
Come to the place for which our fathers sighed?
We have come over a way that with tears has been watered,
We have come, treading our path through the blood of the slaughtered,
Out from the gloomy past,
Till now we stand at last
Where the white gleam of our bright star is cast.
God of our weary years,
God of our silent tears,
Thou who hast brought us thus far on the way;
Thou who hast by Thy might
Led us into the light,
Keep us forever in the path, we pray.
Lest our feet stray from the places, our God, where we met Thee,
Lest, our hearts drunk with the wine of the world, we forget Thee;
Shadowed beneath Thy hand,
May we forever stand,
True to our God,
True to our native land.

title: I Am!
author: John Clare
lines: I am! yet what I am none cares or knows,
My friends forsake me like a memory lost;
I am the self-consumer of my woes—
They rise and vanish in oblivious host,
Like shadows in love's frenzied, stifled throes,
And yet I am, and live—like vapours tost.
Into the nothingness of scorn and noise,
Into the living sea of waking dreams,
Where there is neither sense of life or joys,
But the vast shipwreck of my life's esteems;
Even the dearest that I love the best
Are strange—nay, rather stranger than the rest.
I long for scenes where man hath never trod,
A place where woman never smiled or wept,
There to abide with my Creator, God,
And sleep as I in childhood sweetly slept,
Untroubling and untroubled where I lie,
The grass below—above the vaulted sky.

title: A Litany in Time of Plague
author: Thomas Nashe
lines: Adieu, farewell earth's bliss,
This world uncertain is,
Fond are life's lustful joys,
Death proves them all but toys,
None from his darts can fly,
I am sick, I must die:
Lord, have mercy on us.
Rich men, trust not in wealth,
Gold cannot buy you health,
Physic himself must fade,
All things to end are made,
The plague full swift goes by,
I am sick, I must die:
Lord, have mercy on us.
Beauty is but a flower
Which wrinkles will devour,
Brightness falls from the air,
Queens have died young and fair,
Dust hath closed Helen's eye,
I am sick, I must die:
Lord, have mercy on us.
Strength stoops unto the grave,
Worms feed on Hector brave,
Swords may not fight with fate,
Earth still holds ope her gate,
Come, come! the bells do cry,
I am sick, I must die:
Lord, have mercy on us.

title: Sea Fever
author: John Masefield
lines: I must go down to the seas again, to the lonely sea and the sky,
And all I ask is a tall ship and a star to steer her by;
And the wheel's kick and the wind's song and the white sail's shaking,
And a grey mist on the sea's face, and a grey dawn breaking.
I must go down to the seas again, for the call of the running tide
Is a wild call and a clear call that may not be denied;
And all I ask is a windy day with the white clouds flying,
And the flung spray and the blown spume, and the sea-gulls crying.
I must go down to the seas again, to the vagrant gypsy life,
To the gull's way and the whale's way where the wind's like a whetted knife;
And all I ask is a merry yarn from a laughing fellow-rover,
And quiet sleep and a sweet dream when the long trick's over.

title: I ask for a moment’s indulgence to sit
author: Rabindranath Tagore
lines: I ask for a moment’s indulgence to sit by thy side. The works that I
have in hand I will finish afterwards.
Away from the sight of thy face my heart knows no rest nor respite, and
my work becomes an endless toil in a shoreless sea of toil.
Today the summer has come at my window with its sighs and murmurs; and
the bees are plying their minstrelsy at the court of the flowering
grove.
Now it is time to sit quite, face to face with thee, and to sing
dedication of life in this silent and overflowing leisure.
stanzas: I ask for a moment’s indulgence to sit by thy side. The works that I
have in hand I will finish afterwards.

my work becomes an endless toil in a shoreless sea of toil.

title: Leave this chanting and singing and telling of
author: Rabindranath Tagore
lines: Leave this chanting and singing and telling of beads! Whom dost thou
worship in this lonely dark corner of a temple with doors all shut?
Open thine eyes and see thy God is not before thee!
He is there where the tiller is tilling the hard ground and where the
pathmaker is breaking stones. He is with them in sun and in shower, and
his garment is covered with dust. Put of thy holy mantle and even like
him come down on the dusty soil!
Deliverance? Where is this deliverance to be found? Our master himself
has joyfully taken upon him the bonds of creation; he is bound with us
all for ever.
Come out of thy meditations and leave aside thy flowers and incense!
What harm is there if thy clothes become tattered and stained? Meet him
and stand by him in toil and in sweat of thy brow.

Open thine eyes and see thy God is not before thee!

pathmaker is breaking stones. He is with them in sun and in shower, and

and stand by him in toil and in sweat of thy brow.

title: The time that my journey takes is long
author: Rabindranath Tagore
lines: The time that my journey takes is long and the way of it long.
I came out on the chariot of the first gleam of light, and pursued my
voyage through the wildernesses of worlds leaving my track on many a
star and planet.
It is the most distant course that comes nearest to thyself, and that
training is the most intricate which leads to the utter simplicity of a
tune.
The traveller has to knock at every alien door to come to his own, and
one has to wander through all the outer worlds to reach the innermost
shrine at the end.
My eyes strayed far and wide before I shut them and said “Here art
thou!”
The question and the cry “Oh, where?” melt into tears of a thousand
streams and deluge the world with the flood of the assurance “I am!”

title: The song that I came to sing remains
author: Rabindranath Tagore
lines: The song that I came to sing remains unsung to this day.
I have spent my days in stringing and in unstringing my instrument.
The time has not come true, the words have not been rightly set; only
there is the agony of wishing in my heart.
The blossom has not opened; only the wind is sighing by.
I have not seen his face, nor have I listened to his voice; only I have
heard his gentle footsteps from the road before my house.
The livelong day has passed in spreading his seat on the floor; but the
lamp has not been lit and I cannot ask him into my house.
I live in the hope of meeting with him; but this meeting is not yet.
stanzas: The song that I came to sing remains unsung to this day.
I have spent my days in stringing and in unstringing my instrument.
The time has not come true, the words have not been rightly set; only
there is the agony of wishing in my heart.

I live in the hope of meeting with him; but this meeting is not yet.

title: My desires are many and my cry is
author: Rabindranath Tagore
lines: My desires are many and my cry is pitiful, but ever didst thou save me
by hard refusals; and this strong mercy has been wrought into my life
through and through.
Day by day thou art making me worthy of the simple, great gifts that
thou gavest to me unasked—this sky and the light, this body and the
life and the mind—saving me from perils of overmuch desire.
There are times when I languidly linger and times when I awaken and
hurry in search of my goal; but cruelly thou hidest thyself from before
me.
Day by day thou art making me worthy of thy full acceptance by refusing
me ever and anon, saving me from perils of weak, uncertain desire.
stanzas: My desires are many and my cry is pitiful, but ever didst thou save me
by hard refusals; and this strong mercy has been wrought into my life
through and through.

title: I am only waiting for love to give
author: Rabindranath Tagore
lines: I am only waiting for love to give myself up at last into his hands.
That is why it is so late and why I have been guilty of such omissions.
They come with their laws and their codes to bind me fast; but I evade
them ever, for I am only waiting for love to give myself up at last
into his hands.
People blame me and call me heedless; I doubt not they are right in
their blame.
The market day is over and work is all done for the busy. Those who
came to call me in vain have gone back in anger. I am only waiting for
love to give myself up at last into his hands.
stanzas: I am only waiting for love to give myself up at last into his hands.
That is why it is so late and why I have been guilty of such omissions.
They come with their laws and their codes to bind me fast; but I evade
them ever, for I am only waiting for love to give myself up at last

People blame me and call me heedless; I doubt not they are right in

title: If thou speakest not I will fill my
author: Rabindranath Tagore
lines: If thou speakest not I will fill my heart with thy silence and endure
it. I will keep still and wait like the night with starry vigil and its
head bent low with patience.
The morning will surely come, the darkness will vanish, and thy voice
pour down in golden streams breaking through the sky.
Then thy words will take wing in songs from every one of my birds’
nests, and thy melodies will break forth in flowers in all my forest
groves.

it. I will keep still and wait like the night with starry vigil and its
head bent low with patience.

Then thy words will take wing in songs from every one of my birds’

groves.

title: I must launch out my boat. The languid
author: Rabindranath Tagore
lines: I must launch out my boat. The languid hours pass by on the shore—Alas
for me!
The spring has done its flowering and taken leave. And now with the
burden of faded futile flowers I wait and linger.
The waves have become clamorous, and upon the bank in the shady lane
the yellow leaves flutter and fall.
What emptiness do you gaze upon! Do you not feel a thrill passing
through the air with the notes of the far-away song floating from the
other shore?
stanzas: I must launch out my boat. The languid hours pass by on the shore—Alas
for me!
The spring has done its flowering and taken leave. And now with the

title: Where the mind is without fear and the
author: Rabindranath Tagore
lines: Where the mind is without fear and the head is held high;
Where knowledge is free;
Where the world has not been broken up into fragments by narrow
domestic walls;
Where words come out from the depth of truth;
Where tireless striving stretches its arms towards perfection;
Where the clear stream of reason has not lost its way into the dreary
desert sand of dead habit;
Where the mind is led forward by thee into ever-widening thought and
action—
Into that heaven of freedom, my Father, let my country awake.
stanzas: Where the mind is without fear and the head is held high;
Where knowledge is free;
Where the world has not been broken up into fragments by narrow
domestic walls;
Where words come out from the depth of truth;
Where tireless striving stretches its arms towards perfection;
Where the clear stream of reason has not lost its way into the dreary
desert sand of dead habit;
Where the mind is led forward by thee into ever-widening thought and
action—
Into that heaven of freedom, my Father, let my country awake.
excerpt: Where the mind is without fear and the head is held high; Where knowledge is free;

title: This is my prayer to thee, my lord
author: Rabindranath Tagore
lines: This is my prayer to thee, my lord—strike, strike at the root of penury
in my heart.
Give me the strength lightly to bear my joys and sorrows.
Give me the strength to make my love fruitful in service.
Give me the strength never to disown the poor or bend my knees before
insolent might.
Give me the strength to raise my mind high above daily trifles.
And give me the strength to surrender my strength to thy will with
love.
stanzas: This is my prayer to thee, my lord—strike, strike at the root of penury
in my heart.
Give me the strength lightly to bear my joys and sorrows.
Give me the strength to make my love fruitful in service.
Give me the strength never to disown the poor or bend my knees before
insolent might.
Give me the strength to raise my mind high above daily trifles.
And give me the strength to surrender my strength to thy will with
love.
excerpt: This is my prayer to thee, my lord—strike, strike at the root of penury in my heart.

title: When the heart is hard and parched up
author: Rabindranath Tagore
lines: When the heart is hard and parched up, come upon me with a shower of
mercy.
When grace is lost from life, come with a burst of song.
When tumultuous work raises its din on all sides shutting me out from
beyond, come to me, my lord of silence, with thy peace and rest.
When my beggarly heart sits crouched, shut up in a corner, break open
the door, my king, and come with the ceremony of a king.
When desire blinds the mind with delusion and dust, O thou holy one,
thou wakeful, come with thy light and thy thunder.
stanzas: When the heart is hard and parched up, come upon me with a shower of
mercy.
When grace is lost from life, come with a burst of song.
When tumultuous work raises its din on all sides shutting me out from
beyond, come to me, my lord of silence, with thy peace and rest.
When my beggarly heart sits crouched, shut up in a corner, break open
the door, my king, and come with the ceremony of a king.
When desire blinds the mind with delusion and dust, O thou holy one,
thou wakeful, come with thy light and thy thunder.
excerpt: When the heart is hard and parched up, come upon me with a shower of mercy.

title: Have you not heard his silent steps? He
author: Rabindranath Tagore
lines: Have you not heard his silent steps? He comes, comes, ever comes.
Every moment and every age, every day and every night he comes, comes,
ever comes.
Many a song have I sung in many a mood of mind, but all their notes
have always proclaimed, “He comes, comes, ever comes.”
In the fragrant days of sunny April through the forest path he comes,
comes, ever comes.
In the rainy gloom of July nights on the thundering chariot of clouds
he comes, comes, ever comes.
In sorrow after sorrow it is his steps that press upon my heart, and it
is the golden touch of his feet that makes my joy to shine.
stanzas: Have you not heard his silent steps? He comes, comes, ever comes.
Every moment and every age, every day and every night he comes, comes,
ever comes.
Many a song have I sung in many a mood of mind, but all their notes
have always proclaimed, “He comes, comes, ever comes.”
In the fragrant days of sunny April through the forest path he comes,
comes, ever comes.
In the rainy gloom of July nights on the thundering chariot of clouds
he comes, comes, ever comes.
In sorrow after sorrow it is his steps that press upon my heart, and it
is the golden touch of his feet that makes my joy to shine.
excerpt: Have you not heard his silent steps? He comes, comes, ever comes. Every moment and every age, every day and every night he comes, comes,

title: The morning sea of silence broke into ripples
author: Rabindranath Tagore
lines: The morning sea of silence broke into ripples of bird songs; and the
flowers were all merry by the roadside; and the wealth of gold was
scattered through the rift of the clouds while we busily went on our
way and paid no heed.
We sang no glad songs nor played; we went not to the village for
barter; we spoke not a word nor smiled; we lingered not on the way. We
quickened our pace more and more as the time sped by.
The sun rose to the mid sky and doves cooed in the shade. Withered
leaves danced and whirled in the hot air of noon. The shepherd boy
drowsed and dreamed in the shadow of the banyan tree, and I laid myself
down by the water and stretched my tired limbs on the grass.
My companions laughed at me in scorn; they held their heads high and
hurried on; they never looked back nor rested; they vanished in the
distant blue haze. They crossed many meadows and hills, and passed
through strange, far-away countries. All honour to you, heroic host of
the interminable path! Mockery and reproach pricked me to rise, but
found no response in me. I gave myself up for lost in the depth of a
glad humiliation—in the shadow of a dim delight.
The repose of the sun-embroidered green gloom slowly spread over my
heart. I forgot for what I had travelled, and I surrendered my mind
without struggle to the maze of shadows and songs.
At last, when I woke from my slumber and opened my eyes, I saw thee
standing by me, flooding my sleep with thy smile. How I had feared that
the path was long and wearisome, and the struggle to reach thee was
hard!

flowers were all merry by the roadside; and the wealth of gold was
scattered through the rift of the clouds while we busily went on our
way and paid no heed.
We sang no glad songs nor played; we went not to the village for
barter; we spoke not a word nor smiled; we lingered not on the way. We
quickened our pace more and more as the time sped by.

title: I had gone a-begging from door to door
author: Rabindranath Tagore
lines: I had gone a-begging from door to door in the village path, when thy
golden chariot appeared in the distance like a gorgeous dream and I
wondered who was this King of all kings!
My hopes rose high and methought my evil days were at an end, and I
stood waiting for alms to be given unasked and for wealth scattered on
all sides in the dust.
The chariot stopped where I stood. Thy glance fell on me and thou
camest down with a smile. I felt that the luck of my life had come at
last. Then of a sudden thou didst hold out thy right hand and say “What
hast thou to give to me?”
Ah, what a kingly jest was it to open thy palm to a beggar to beg! I
was confused and stood undecided, and then from my wallet I slowly took
out the least little grain of corn and gave it to thee.
But how great my surprise when at the day’s end I emptied my bag on the
floor to find a least little gram of gold among the poor heap. I
bitterly wept and wished that I had had the heart to give thee my all.

golden chariot appeared in the distance like a gorgeous dream and I
wondered who was this King of all kings!

title: Thou hast made me known to friends whom
author: Rabindranath Tagore
lines: Thou hast made me known to friends whom I knew not. Thou hast given me
seats in homes not my own. Thou hast brought the distant near and made
a brother of the stranger.
I am uneasy at heart when I have to leave my accustomed shelter; I
forget that there abides the old in the new, and that there also thou
abidest.
Through birth and death, in this world or in others, wherever thou
leadest me it is thou, the same, the one companion of my endless life
who ever linkest my heart with bonds of joy to the unfamiliar.
When one knows thee, then alien there is none, then no door is shut.
Oh, grant me my prayer that I may never lose the bliss of the touch of
the one in the play of many.

seats in homes not my own. Thou hast brought the distant near and made
a brother of the stranger.

title: The same stream of life that runs through
author: Rabindranath Tagore
lines: The same stream of life that runs through my veins night and day runs
through the world and dances in rhythmic measures.
It is the same life that shoots in joy through the dust of the earth in
numberless blades of grass and breaks into tumultuous waves of leaves
and flowers.
It is the same life that is rocked in the ocean-cradle of birth and of
death, in ebb and in flow.
I feel my limbs are made glorious by the touch of this world of life.
And my pride is from the life-throb of ages dancing in my blood this
moment.
stanzas: The same stream of life that runs through my veins night and day runs
through the world and dances in rhythmic measures.
It is the same life that shoots in joy through the dust of the earth in
numberless blades of grass and breaks into tumultuous waves of leaves
and flowers.
It is the same life that is rocked in the ocean-cradle of birth and of
death, in ebb and in flow.
I feel my limbs are made glorious by the touch of this world of life.
And my pride is from the life-throb of ages dancing in my blood this
moment.
excerpt: The same stream of life that runs through my veins night and day runs through the world and dances in rhythmic measures.

title: Deliverance is not for me in renunciation. I
author: Rabindranath Tagore
lines: Deliverance is not for me in renunciation. I feel the embrace of
freedom in a thousand bonds of delight.
Thou ever pourest for me the fresh draught of thy wine of various
colours and fragrance, filling this earthen vessel to the brim.
My world will light its hundred different lamps with thy flame and
place them before the altar of thy temple.
No, I will never shut the doors of my senses. The delights of sight and
hearing and touch will bear thy delight.
Yes, all my illusions will burn into illumination of joy, and all my
desires ripen into fruits of love.
stanzas: Deliverance is not for me in renunciation. I feel the embrace of
freedom in a thousand bonds of delight.
Thou ever pourest for me the fresh draught of thy wine of various
colours and fragrance, filling this earthen vessel to the brim.
My world will light its hundred different lamps with thy flame and

Yes, all my illusions will burn into illumination of joy, and all my

excerpt: Deliverance is not for me in renunciation. I feel the embrace of freedom in a thousand bonds of delight.

title: Time is endless in thy hands, my lord
author: Rabindranath Tagore
lines: Time is endless in thy hands, my lord. There is none to count thy
minutes.
Days and nights pass and ages bloom and fade like flowers. Thou knowest
how to wait.
Thy centuries follow each other perfecting a small wild flower.
We have no time to lose, and having no time we must scramble for a
chances. We are too poor to be late.
And thus it is that time goes by while I give it to every querulous man
who claims it, and thine altar is empty of all offerings to the last.
At the end of the day I hasten in fear lest thy gate to be shut; but I
find that yet there is time.
stanzas: Time is endless in thy hands, my lord. There is none to count thy
minutes.
Days and nights pass and ages bloom and fade like flowers. Thou knowest
how to wait.
Thy centuries follow each other perfecting a small wild flower.
We have no time to lose, and having no time we must scramble for a
chances. We are too poor to be late.
And thus it is that time goes by while I give it to every querulous man

find that yet there is time.
excerpt: Time is endless in thy hands, my lord. There is none to count thy minutes.

title: I was not aware of the moment when
author: Rabindranath Tagore
lines: I was not aware of the moment when I first crossed the threshold of
this life.
What was the power that made me open out into this vast mystery like a
bud in the forest at midnight!
When in the morning I looked upon the light I felt in a moment that I
was no stranger in this world, that the inscrutable without name and
form had taken me in its arms in the form of my own mother.
Even so, in death the same unknown will appear as ever known to me. And
because I love this life, I know I shall love death as well.
The child cries out when from the right breast the mother takes it
away, in the very next moment to find in the left one its consolation.
stanzas: I was not aware of the moment when I first crossed the threshold of
this life.
What was the power that made me open out into this vast mystery like a
bud in the forest at midnight!
When in the morning I looked upon the light I felt in a moment that I
was no stranger in this world, that the inscrutable without name and
form had taken me in its arms in the form of my own mother.
Even so, in death the same unknown will appear as ever known to me. And

away, in the very next moment to find in the left one its consolation.

title: When I go from hence let this be
author: Rabindranath Tagore
lines: When I go from hence let this be my parting word, that what I have seen
is unsurpassable.
I have tasted of the hidden honey of this lotus that expands on the
ocean of light, and thus am I blessed—let this be my parting word.
In this playhouse of infinite forms I have had my play and here have I
caught sight of him that is formless.
My whole body and my limbs have thrilled with his touch who is beyond
touch; and if the end comes here, let it come—let this be my parting
word.
stanzas: When I go from hence let this be my parting word, that what I have seen
is unsurpassable.
I have tasted of the hidden honey of this lotus that expands on the
ocean of light, and thus am I blessed—let this be my parting word.
In this playhouse of infinite forms I have had my play and here have I
caught sight of him that is formless.
My whole body and my limbs have thrilled with his touch who is beyond
touch; and if the end comes here, let it come—let this be my parting
word.
excerpt: When I go from hence let this be my parting word, that what I have seen is unsurpassable.
